journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Directly adopting inverse biosensors to image live cell enzyme activities in nanodomains
abstract: Many biochemical pathways can be monitored by outfitting molecular switches with reporting mechanisms such as fluorescence. The output of these biosensors can either increase or decrease upon target activation. Both types can report average relative changes in time. But a naïve imaging of inverse biosensors, which gives readout decrease, will form nonsensical images by giving low values to both the background and foreground. Thus, currently, superresolution enzyme activity imaging cannot follow the actions of those enzymes that require inverse biosensing. This is a significant obstacle for understanding the ways cells organize their signaling via nanodomains and compartments. We break this barrier and rationally develop a genetically encoded principle to quantify inverse biosensors at superresolution. We generate 3 distinct readout pairs and systematically illustrate previously hidden insights on 3 dynamic signaling hubs.
discussion: DiscussionReadout inversion is fundamental to quantitative image formation but has not been considered in functional superresolution imaging. The Dronpa-TagRFP protein barrel interaction generates suppression in all cases examined except TagRFP-T, because the latter is the only member with a fast-cycling cis-trans state. As a principle, FINICI is not restricted to fluctuating output and should suppress other characteristics inherent to these fluorescent proteins. The E. quadricolor lineage is the third most varied fluorescent protein family, and we showcased the utility of three members. It avoids the labor-intensive screening to find positive biosensors for each inverse switch, which must be done if the reporting unit remains positive. Across distinct biochemistries, we showed that the sensitivity of FINICI matches and can exceed that of FRET. It is genetically encodable, does not require the introduction of exogenous dyes or enzymes, and is sustainable over long-term superresolution activity imaging. The flexibilities introduced here to superresolution activity imaging (inverse switch compatibility, multiple colors, multiple pairs for optimization) were previously limited to single-color- and FRET-based biosensing.Data showed that FINICI can reveal signaling dynamics at superresolution. We have outlined and shown the steps for adopting new inverse switches. Each FINICI biosensor reported here was optimized over a range of excitation laser intensities with appropriate controls to validate responses. Where applicable, appropriate filtering is applied to isolate the fluctuation phase containing statistical information. FINICI superresolution images were directly used in quantitative analyses; normalized, average drug-stimulated activity was corrected with appropriate no-stimulation controls performed under the same cell types, conditions, and imaging parameters. While the normalized pcSOFI response is independent of biosensor expression, very high expression is unnecessary and to aid signal-to-noise, cells with poor expression of the biosensor should be avoided. The reported 51-s temporal resolution was limited here by resource/throughput; ~30 s time resolution was often achieved. When using the fast protocol for FINICI-SrcAR, the photoswitching phase may give complexities; but dynamic imaging remains possible by tuning a higher excitation intensity to optimally cycle the biosensors (SI Appendix, Fig. S15). Of note, attempts at basal-state minimization with a long linker made these biosensors less reliable in FINICI readout. To further enhance a given FINICI biosensor, optimizing the switch orientation relative to the RFP will likely give the best return.The plasma membrane association of Src kinase has long been linked to the behaviors of cell lines from small-cell-lung , breast , leukemic , and other human cancers. Src translocates in early cell adhesion stages for mouse neonatal brain and embryonic fibroblasts , and colocalizes with rafts upon thrombin receptor and fibrinogen activation in platelets . Furthermore, there is evidence that translocation is used to control activated Src. For example, recruitment by raft-resident Cbp anchors suppress c-Src phosphorylation and inhibited c-Src induced transformation ; cells mobilize GM3 gangliosides to modify the membrane and inhibit EGFR-Src signaling ; and an artificial translocation of Src into rafts induces a delamination phenotype through STAT3/MMP . The superresolution visualization reported here will complement tools such as optogenetics to confirm the precise spatiotemporal control of active Src.Our imaging supports the literature and directly showed how cGMP compartmentalization is stimulation dependent. Studies showed that the presence of sGC at the plasma membrane increases NO sensitivity, leading to a significant amount of cGMP at the membrane . Our results suggested that SNO-stimulated cGMP drives less compartment weakening, and sGCs could be more exposed to PDE-driven antagonism. In addition to PDE regulations, the compartment weakening from ANP may result from positive feedback of ANP-induced recruitment of PKG to the membrane that amplifies the guanyl cyclase activity of NPR1/NPRA . A different spatial cGMP control was visualized here for CNP-stimulated NPR2/NPRB, which is known to drive distinct functions in cardiomyocytes . Overall, we believe this perspective hints at an upstream-centric nature of cGMP signaling, which may be rationalized by the relatively low number of bona fide G Kinase Anchoring Proteins reported compared to the variety of A Kinase Anchoring Proteins (AKAP) .Our visualization of Syk is at the substrate phosphorylation level. While dectin-1 can form clusters , we showed that the zymosan-induced active Syk localization pattern was not dectin-1 but actin-centric, compartmentalized near downstream partners and away from initiating receptors. This location is consistent with the role of Syk in phagocytosis . Fc receptors (FcR), another Syk upstream activator, is also clustered and Syk similarly affects actin redistribution after FcR activation through feedback . Thus, despite membrane ITAM clustering during Syk activation, its functional location change, and there may be unidentified Syk anchoring proteins and/or Syk translocation mechanisms like those known for Src. Alternatively, active Syk may cause actin polymerization , and actin features may represent upstream determinants of the subsequent stimulated Syk response. FINICI-SykAR will help visualize and differentiate between these models.
figure_legends: Fluctuation Increase Negated by Intra-Chain Interaction (FINICI) adopts inverse biosensors for superresolution activity imaging. (A) Top: Domain structure of the prototypical FINICI-SrcAR biosensor and its schematic designed function before and after stimulation. Bottom: single pixel fluctuations, normalized to initial fluorescence, from a live HeLa cell expressing wild-type (WT) FINICI-SrcAR before (Left) and after (Right) huEGF stimulation; the center panel shows the increased mean/spread of the intensity distribution after huEGF. (B) Whole cell averaged fluorescence intensity traces before normalization (Right) and after normalization to timepoint 0, just before stimulation (Left). Intensity traces captured continuously for 30 s, every 5.5 min are shown with the intervening time omitted because there was no exposures/imaging; “timepoints” are used to count the sequence of pcSOFI dataset. In the Left panel: the response profile of FINICI-SrcAR includes a clear gain in fluctuation phase in WT-expressing, responding cells. This gain contributes to normalized pcSOFI response (arrow, compare responding and nonresponding), is phosphorylation dependent (compare WT and nonphosphorylatable tyrosine-to-glycine mutant YG), is stimulation dependent (compare WT and No-EGF control); and in Right panel, the response is not correlated with probe expression as shown by raw fluorescence intensity (compare WT No-EGF, WT nonresponding and YG). The increased fluctuation recovery phase is emphasized by gray shade. (C) Superresolved FINICI-SrcAR normalized pcSOFI images in live cell expressing WT biosensor (Left, purple tab) or YG-control (Right, gray tab); warmer colors show higher value, and more visible changes are marked by white arrows. (Scale bar, 10 μm.) (D) Mean time-normalized pcSOFI response time courses show change in normalized pcSOFI values due to huEGF stimulated Src activity with various kinetics. Live HeLa cells expressing WT and YG control biosensor upon 100 ng/mL huEGF stimulation (Left), WT biosensor with/without 100 ng/mL huEGF stimulation (middle), and WT biosensor with/without 50 ng/mL huEGF stimulation (right, upper row). WT biosensor with 10 and 20 µM PP2 pretreatment showed effective suppression and most cells did not show 100 ng/mL huEGF stimulated response (Right, lower row). Two-tailed Welch’s t test results are reported for significance of experimental normalized pcSOFI values at the given time point compared to the reference value marked “ref” in each category, in color-coded manner. t test results are marked as *P ≤ 0.05, **P ≤ 0.01, ***P ≤ 0.001, and ****P ≤ 0.0001; for simplification, nonsignificant comparisons were not marked. (E) Mean normalized FRET-based biosensing time courses observed in live HeLa cells expressing WT biosensor upon treatment with EGF (100 ng/mL). (F) Scanlines at the same location across normalized pcSOFI images before (Left image, black lines) and 5.5 min after stimulation (Right image, red lines) show clear activation of a priori compartmentalized Src (both scanlines i and ii) and newly emerged compartments (line iii, ~9 μm mark) upon huEGF (100 ng/mL). The overall resolution of the activity images was approx. 162 nm (Fourier ring correlation, FRC plug-in v1.0.2, Fiji).

Ordered lipid raft domains harbor transient highly activated Src kinases. (A) Top: normalized pcSOFI images of live HeLa cells expressing WT raft-targeted biosensor before/after huEGF stimulation; microdomains with enhanced activity poststimulation in yellow/white pseudocolors are further marked by white arrows. Bottom: live HeLa cells expressing WT raft-targeted biosensor but without stimulation, no changes were observed. Scanlines show specific resolved features; the overall resolution was ~166 nm (FRC plug-in v1.0.2, Fiji). (B) Mean time-normalized pcSOFI time courses on rafts of live HeLa cells showing two types of kinetics upon stimulation and no response without stimulation. (C) Mean normalized FRET-based SrcAR biosensing time courses on rafts of live HeLa cells show two types of kinetics upon stimulation, mirroring raft-targeted FINICI-SrcAR. (D) Violin plots of time-normalized pcSOFI values after huEGF stimulation (t = 5.5 min) across cells expressing either (ordered lipid) raft-targeted (magenta) and (disordered lipid) non-raft-targeted (blue) biosensor, separated by response type, where Src activity is significantly higher in rafts but only for transient cases. (E and F) Violin plot results of particle analyses on the number of and normalized pcSOFI value within active Src microdomains in cells expressing (E) raft-targeted and (F) non-raft-targeted FINICI-SrcAR. Specifically for diameters 156/247 nm representing small/large microdomains. The Two-tailed Welch’s t test results are reported for the significance of experimental normalized pcSOFI values at the given time point compared to the reference value marked “ref” in each category or the indicated pair in each panel in color-coded manner. t test results are marked as nonsignificant (ns), *P ≤ 0.05, **P ≤ 0.01, and ***P ≤ 0.001.

cGMP compartmentalization is weakened significantly upon both chemical and receptor-stimulated second messenger production. (A) Domain structure of FINICI-cGi and resultant whole cell averaged fluorescence intensity traces before normalization (Right) and after normalization to timepoint 0, just before stimulation (Left). In Left panel: the response profile of FINICI-cGi lacked sharp ON–OFF behavior but retained gain in fluctuation (arrow, compare WT responding and no-drug control); and in Right panel, the response is not correlated with probe expression as shown by raw fluorescence intensity (compare WT responding and no-drug control). Gray shaded regions are provided to visualize the substantial increase in the fluctuation recovery phase. (B) Mean time-normalized pcSOFI response time courses show kinetic changes in normalized pcSOFI values upon Atrial Natriuretic Peptide (ANP) or Spermine NONOate (SNO) stimulation on live HEK293 cells expressing FINICI-cGi. Two-tailed Welch’s t test results are reported for significance of experimental normalized pcSOFI values at the given time point compared to the reference value (No drug) marked “ref” in each category, in a color-coded manner. (C) Superresolved FINICI-cGi normalized pcSOFI time-lapse images in live HEK293 cell; magenta colors show higher value, and more visible changes are marked by white arrows; scale bars: 10 μm. Note that basal microdomains exist prior to stimulation (t = 0 min); red, blue, and colorless frame indicates ANP, SNO, and no-drug cases, respectively. (D) The corresponding compartment and pixel fraction coverage analyses from each type of treatment (ANP, SNO, no-drug; color coding as in C) showing the manner in which compartmentalization weakened was through cGMP diffusion, most readily affecting microdomains of smaller diameter. Two-way ANOVA results between 3 treatment conditions and 3 time points are reported compared to the reference value marked “ref” in each category, in color-coded manner with significance marked. For Left panels reporting compartment decrease kinetics with respect to microdomain diameter, ANP/SNO treatments were significantly different from each of no-drug reference at **** until 119 nm, at * until 143 nm, and nonsignificant for larger microdomains; ANP treated microdomains were significantly different from SNO treated microdomains at * for diameters 90 and 111 nm (blue asterisks in ANP panel). For right panels reporting pixel fraction, significance is as marked and no significant difference was found between ANP/SNO treatments at 5.5 or 11 min. (E) Schematic for cGMP compartment weakening from quantifications. Where applicable, ANOVA or two-tailed Welch’s t test results are marked as *P ≤ 0.05, **P ≤ 0.01, ***P ≤ 0.001, and ****P ≤ 0.0001; for simplification, nonsignificant were not marked.

Fast FINICI-cGi kymographs directly captured compartment weakening. Left of each panel: comparing t = 0 and 306 s after (A) ANP- and (B) SNO-stimulation. Right of each panel: kymographs, with time in seconds on the x-axes, magenta lines demarcating 0 and 306 s time, and spatial distance in the y-axes. Pseudocolor I scales from cold (cyan/green) colors to warm (orange/red). Therefore, prior to stimulation, baseline cGMP was low in pcSOFI values and appeared in cyan/green. Over the stimulation time course, warmer colors (orange/red) appeared in both punctate and diffuse fashion, directly showing how cGMP nanodomains were progressively overwhelmed by diffuse cGMP.

Activated Syk kinase is found around its downstream substrate regions instead of upstream receptors. (A) Domain structure of FINICI-SykAR. (B) Mean whole cell averaged normalized pcSOFI response time courses from live RAW264.7 cells expressing FINICI-SykAR show kinetic changes in Syk activity upon stimulation with two different zymosan dosage (Left, 5 μg/mL; Right, 10 μg/mL) in solid lines. In the rightmost panel, three types of controls all showed no response upon 10 μg/mL zymosan stimulation: HEK293 cells lacking dectin-1 receptor (dashed lines) expressing the WT biosensor, RAW264.7 cells expressing the phospho-dead YG mutant biosensor, and RAW264.7 cells expressing the WT biosensor but pretreated with Syk inhibitor R788 at 5 μM. (C) zymosan dosage significantly affects Syk kinetics and 10 μg/mL dosage caused longer activation. (D) Normalized pcSOFI time-lapse images in live cell expressing FINICI-SykAR. Top row: RAW264.7 cell white/magenta colors show higher values that emerge in actin-rich regions such as filopodia and the cell cortex after zymosan; overall resolution was ~164 nm (FRC plug-in v1.0.2, Fiji). Bottom row: control HEK293 cell lacking dectin-1 expression showed no change in the pcSOFI value response despite stimulation. (Scale bar 10 μm.) (E) Location of active Syk (pcSOFI with FINICI-SykAR) together with localization of f-actin (dSTORM with phalloidin) of the same cell highlights similar filopodia and cell cortex features, illustrating the close correlation between f-actin and activated Syk. Where indicated, two-tailed Welch’s t test results are reported for significance of experimental normalized pcSOFI values at the given time point compared to the reference value marked “ref” in each category, in color-coded manner. Significance is marked as ***P ≤ 0.001 and ****P ≤ 0.0001; nonsignificant were not marked for simplification.
